title: The Earl of March to Henry IV
summary: The checked-in XML is a repository representation of University of Michigan CME00126, an encoding of Hingeston's letter X, 'The Earl of March to Henry IV,' in Royal and Historical Letters volume 1 (1860), printed pp. 23-25. The source unit begins at heading X in the lower half of p. 23 after the preceding letter and translation, and ends after a Middle French endorsement near the top of p. 25 before letter XI. Hingeston transcribed the original paper letter at British Library Cotton MS Vespasian F VII, f. 22, written at Dunbar on 18 February 1400 by George Dunbar, Earl of March. The day and month are internal; the year is explicitly supplied by Hingeston's chronological catalogue and independently recorded in the current BL catalog. A complete 596-page Internet Archive PDF, complete OCR, IA metadata JSON, and exact BL catalog JSON are cached with fixity. No public digital facsimile of f. 22 or the complete codex was found.
review_notes: The primary subject is the repository artifact at CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00126.xml. The medieval letter, Hingeston editorial unit, U-M encoding, original f. 22 document, parent Cotton codex, digital facsimile, and catalog records are separate entities.

The intellectual work is one letter with a Middle French address endorsement. Hingeston's supplied title, marginal summaries, witness note, cross-references, date gloss, and earl-identification note are editorial apparatus.

Every complete-volume cache and the encoding-to-edition relation preserve the spatial boundary: heading X starts in the lower half of printed p. 23 after the preceding letter and translation; the endorsement ends near the top of p. 25 before letter XI begins.

The original document is BL Cotton MS Vespasian F VII, f. 22, not the codex as a whole. The current BL catalog explicitly enumerates it as item 18, calls it original, dates it 18 February 1400 at Dunbar, and places f. 22 within a paper run.

The document's exact written side or sides and present survival of Hingeston's reported seal traces were not verified because the current BL record has no digitised-content link and no public manuscript image was found.

The letter gives the eighteenth day of February but no year. Hingeston's chronological catalogue assigns 1400 after describing his chronological method; the current BL catalog independently gives the same full date.

The primary language is normalized as Older Scots (specifically Early Scots) from current DSL historical periodization. The letter calls its language Englishe; DSL explains that Scots was called Inglis before 1494. U-M and the XML retain a legacy Middle English classification.

Hingeston's footnote on printed p. 24 reads 'See Rymer, viii. 131.' The checked-in XML and current U-M full-text display read '181'; this is preserved as a source/encoding discrepancy, not silently corrected.

Hingeston calls George Dunbar the eleventh earl. A current University of Edinburgh authority record calls George Dunbar (1338-1420) the tenth Earl of Dunbar and March; the preferred name remains ordinal-free.
The current BL physical description reports 130 folios, while an inherited Latin scope statement says 121. Current foliation controls access planning; the discrepancy is retained.

The Internet Archive scan derives from a PIMS-University of Toronto copy, call number 3050475. That physical source copy is distinct from the abstract edition, the scan bytes, and the IA metadata catalog object, even though the schema has no dedicated print-copy entity type.
The pinned CME LICENSE uses Unlicense wording framed as software; its applicability to corpus XML, data, and markup is not assumed.

entities.label: General Corpus repository artifact for CME00126
entities.description: The checked-in ETS XML file in the pinned CME submodule, including TEMPHEAD revision metadata and the complete encoded letter X with editorial apparatus.
notes: IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID agree on CME00126.
TEMPHEAD records a 2016 structural review that removed A.D. 1400 running dates and restructured opener, closer, and tailnote.

entities.label: University of Michigan CME00126 electronic text
alternate_labels: The Earl of March to Henry IV [Royal and Historical Letters, RS 18.1 (1860), no. X]
entities.description: The U-M Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item presenting Hingeston's complete letter X and apparatus. Its provider author string combines the Earl of March and Hingeston; this manifest separates medieval authorship from editorial responsibility.
notes: The provider and root TEXT/@LANG classify the item as Middle English; the work metadata normalizes the 1400 Scottish vernacular as Older Scots while retaining the legacy classification as representation history.
The public full-text display repeats the XML's Rymer viii. 181 reading.

entities.label: Hingeston's 1860 edition of The Earl of March to Henry IV
alternate_labels: Royal and Historical Letters volume 1, letter X
entities.description: Letter X within Hingeston's chronologically arranged documentary edition. The exact unit occupies printed pp. 23-25: it starts at heading X in the lower half of p. 23 after the preceding letter and translation, and ends after the Middle French endorsement near the top of p. 25 before letter XI. Hingeston supplies the English heading, marginal summaries, manuscript/source note, cross-references, feast-date gloss, running date, and signatory note.

bibliographic.citation: F. C. Hingeston, ed., Royal and Historical Letters During the Reign of Henry the Fourth, King of England and of France, and Lord of Ireland, vol. 1, A.D. 1399-1404 (London: Longman, Green, Longman, and Roberts, 1860), letter X, pp. 23-25, Rolls Series 18.1.
bibliographic.title: The Earl of March to Henry IV
bibliographic.container_title: Royal and Historical Letters During the Reign of Henry the Fourth, King of England and of France, and Lord of Ireland

notes: The chronological catalogue on printed p. xcviii explicitly dates this unit 18 February 1400.
The preface says source notes identify material and original/copy status and that missing text is supplied in brackets.
The p. 24 Rymer footnote reads viii. 131, not the XML's 181.

entities.label: London, British Library, Cotton MS Vespasian F VII, f. 22
alternate_labels: Geo. Dunbar, Earl of March, to Henry IV
entities.description: The original physical paper letter from George Dunbar, Earl of March, to Henry IV. The current BL catalog enumerates it as item 18 at f. 22 and dates it Dunbar, 18 February 1400. The exact written side or sides and current seal-trace survival were not publicly verified.

identifiers.value: Cotton MS Vespasian F VII, f. 22

date_statements.label: Letter date
date_statements.notes: The letter itself supplies day and month; the year is supplied by Hingeston's catalogue and the current BL record.

holding.shelfmark: Cotton MS Vespasian F VII, f. 22
historical_designations: MS. Cotton. Vespas. F. VII. fol. 22
holding.notes: The current catalog exposes the letter as item 18 inside the codex-level record rather than as a separately addressable child record.

physical_description.extent: One original letter at f. 22; exact written side or sides not publicly verified
physical_description.format: Document bound within a composite codex
notes: The current BL physical inventory places f. 22 in the paper run ff. 17-27.
Hingeston reports traces of a seal. This historical observation is not promoted to a confirmed present-condition claim without a current image or item-level inspection.
George Dunbar is recorded as the letter's sender and author; no inspected source identifies the person or hand responsible for writing the Middle French address endorsement.

entities.label: London, British Library, Cotton MS Vespasian F VII
entities.description: Composite codex of records and correspondence concerning England and Scotland, 1296-1547, with an early modern contents list at ff. 126-130. It contains the target original at f. 22.

holding.notes: Previously owned by the Cotton family at Conington in the seventeenth century; now British Library Western Manuscripts, Cotton Collection.

physical_description.extent: 130 folios: f. 1 early modern endleaf plus ff. 2-130
notes: Current catalog condition: intact.
British Museum binding dated 1847, repaired at the British Museum in 1950.
An inherited scope statement says the codex consists of 121 leaves; the current structured physical description says 130 and controls present access planning.
The target f. 22 is paper.

entities.label: British Library catalog record 040-001103462 for Cotton MS Vespasian F VII
entities.description: The current ISAD(G) catalog description of the codex. Its Scope and Content item 18 identifies the target as an original letter from Geo. Dunbar, Earl of March, to Henry IV at f. 22, Dunbar, 18 February 1400; its structured fields supply physical description, holding, access, and a blank Digitised Content value.
notes: The exact JSON representation is cached locally with SHA-256 fixity.
The blank Digitised Content field is evidence of no link in this record, not proof that no private, commissioned, or unlinked image exists.

entities.label: Internet Archive digital facsimile of Royal and Historical Letters, volume 1
entities.description: A complete 596-page PDF facsimile with OCR, derived from a PIMS-University of Toronto physical copy, call number 3050475. The target letter occupies printed pp. 23-25, physical PDF pp. 143-145, BookReader leaves n143-n145, with the precise mid-page boundaries stated in access metadata.
entities.reproduction_format: 596-page PDF with complete OCR derivatives; provider metadata reports 598 source images
notes: The PIMS source copy, abstract 1860 edition, digital facsimile bytes, and IA metadata record are distinct layers.
The PDF page count and provider imagecount differ because they measure different digital layers; neither is silently substituted for the other.

notes: This catalog object is distinct from the facsimile and the PIMS physical source copy.

access.access_method: Request Cotton MS Vespasian F VII through the BL online collection-item request form; consultation of manuscripts and archives is in London and requires a current Reader Pass, with item availability and restrictions subject to staff confirmation
access.contact: British Library Reference Services; quote Cotton MS Vespasian F VII
notes: Readers whose pass predates 21 March 2024 must renew before pre-requesting. Restrictions and temporary unavailability may apply.

access.access_method: No public f. 22 image or complete-codex image sequence was found; the current record's Digitised Content value is blank
notes: This finding is time-bound and does not exclude private, commissioned, unlinked, or future images.

access.access_method: British Library online imaging order; provide shelfmark, title, exact folios and sides, desired format, and payment details
access.contact: British Library Imaging Services; permissions@bl.uk for reproduction permission questions
access.format: Expert photography for rare manuscripts; scans are unavailable from original manuscripts
notes: For a complete mediated reproduction request, specify front and back boards, spine, pastedowns, all flyleaves/endleaves and blanks, and both recto and verso of every folio f. 1-130; separately identify target f. 22 recto and verso. Confirm coverage with staff because the order form requires exact folios/sides and the item's condition, availability, and restrictions may limit fulfillment.

rights.component: Underlying 1400 letter text and address endorsement as historical textual works; physical ownership, modern markup, catalog prose, and photographs excluded
rights.basis: The letter was written in 1400 and was published by Hingeston in 1860. Any authorial term and the 1860 publication's typographical term have long expired; the exact U-M item additionally asserts public-domain/No Copyright - United States status. This does not confer rights in the physical document or later photographs.

rights.component: Hingeston's 1860 editorial text, arrangement, and typographical presentation
rights.basis: Hingeston died in 1910 according to provider metadata; the UK term for literary works is life plus 70 years, and published-edition typographical arrangement lasts 25 years. The 1860 edition is outside both terms.

rights.component: University of Michigan CME00126 digital item
rights.basis: The exact item page states that the materials are in the public domain and supplies the DPLA No Copyright - United States rights statement; provider access and service terms remain distinct.

rights.component: Repository-specific XML, markup, and revision metadata
rights.basis: The pinned CME repository contains Unlicense language framed as applying to software. Its application to corpus XML, data, and markup is not explicit enough to broaden into a high-confidence license determination for the complete artifact.
notes: Underlying medieval and 1860 texts are separately modeled as public domain.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan and OCR derivatives of the PIMS-University of Toronto source copy
rights.basis: The exact IA metadata response contains no explicit item rights statement, license URL, possible-copyright-status, or copyright-region field. Public download availability and public-domain source text are not treated as an affirmative license for the scan derivatives.

rights.component: British Library catalog text and structured record data, excluding manuscript images and third-party material
rights.basis: The current BL catalog page footer states that British Library text is available under CC BY except where otherwise stated.

rights.component: Any manuscript photographs supplied through British Library Imaging Services
rights.basis: No item-specific images or open license were found. Current BL terms place responsibility for third-party rights on the requester and prohibit copying or reproducing supplied material without written BL permission, potentially subject to additional fees.
notes: This concerns commissioned image files and contractual reuse, not copyright in the underlying medieval letter.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Original paper letter at Cotton MS Vespasian F VII, f. 22, within a larger miscellany
editorial_practices.representation: Chronologically arranged as letter X with supplied English title, A.D. 1400 running date, marginal summaries, source/status footnote, cross-references, bracketed supply, feast-day gloss, and signatory identification.
editorial_practices.description: Hingeston presents one complete documentary letter and its endorsement inside a larger chronological edition, distinguishing manuscript transcription from editorial access matter through layout and notes.
editorial_practices.scope: Printed pp. 23-25, beginning and ending mid-page

editorial_practices.source_feature: Letter X embedded between neighboring letters on shared printed pages in a 596-page digital volume
editorial_practices.representation: The electronic item encodes heading X through the endorsement, including Hingeston's apparatus, and excludes the preceding translated letter above it on p. 23 and letter XI below it on p. 25.
editorial_practices.description: CME00126 is complete relative to Hingeston's exact editorial unit and selective relative to the printed volume and shared boundary pages.
editorial_practices.scope: Complete CME00126 item relative to printed pp. 23-25

evidence.citation: CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00126.xml at pinned CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for artifact identity, encoded text and apparatus, XML structure, language declaration, revision note, signature, endorsement, and the encoded Rymer 181 reading.

evidence.citation: Local structural and textual audit of CME00126.xml against Hingeston's 1860 page images.
evidence.summary: Verified IDs, exact source boundary, one letter DIV1, two HEAD, eight NOTE, eight P, four PB, opener, closer, and tailnote; confirmed that the XML's Rymer viii. 181 differs from printed viii. 131.
Unlicense text framed as applying to software; it does not explicitly resolve scope over XML, corpus data, markup, underlying editions, or manuscript images.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, 'The Earl of March to Henry IV,' CME00126.
evidence.summary: Official item page confirming the established title and Hingeston source, public electronic text, combined provider author field, public-domain statement, and No Copyright - United States rights URI; full text preserves the Rymer 181 reading.

evidence.citation: F. C. Hingeston, ed., Royal and Historical Letters During the Reign of Henry the Fourth, vol. 1 (London, 1860), title page; preface pp. xiii-xiv; chronological catalogue p. xcviii; letter X, pp. 23-25.
evidence.locator: Title page PDF p. 11; preface PDF pp. 21-22; chronological catalogue PDF p. 106; target printed pp. 23-25 = physical PDF pp. 143-145 = BookReader n143-n145; OCR lines 6926-7011
evidence.summary: Exact complete source volume, visually inspected for title and publication, editorial method, explicit catalogue date 18 February 1400, target text and apparatus, original-paper-f.22 statement, seal-trace report, exact mid-page boundaries, and printed Rymer viii. 131.
notes: The hash is for the complete cached PDF. qpdf reports 596 pages, no encryption, and no syntax or stream errors.

evidence.summary: Records title, volume 1, 1860 date, Hingeston and Public Record Office creators, Longman publication, PIMS-University of Toronto source copy call number 3050475, University of Toronto sponsor, 598 source images, Toronto scanning, and derivative files; contains no explicit rights or license fields.
notes: The hash is for the exact cached metadata JSON response.

evidence.citation: British Library, Cotton MS Vespasian F VII, record 040-001103462.
evidence.locator: Scope and Content item 18, f. 22; structured physical characteristics; Access; Digitised Content
evidence.summary: Current official record confirming item 18 as the original George Dunbar letter at f. 22, dated Dunbar 18 February 1400; paper support for f. 22; 130-folio current extent; dimensions, condition, binding, access restrictions, blank digitised-content field, and CC BY footer license for BL text.
notes: The hash is for the exact cached JSON representation. The CC BY statement was verified on the corresponding HTML page footer.

evidence.citation: University of Edinburgh Library Heritage Collections, La.V/29, creator authority 'Dunbar, George, 1338-1420 (10th Earl of Dunbar and March).'
evidence.summary: Current institutional authority evidence for the tenth-earl numbering convention, conflicting with Hingeston's eleventh-earl note.

evidence.citation: Jeremy Smith, 'Scots: an outline history,' Dictionaries of the Scots Language (2017), Origins and Dominance of Scots.
evidence.summary: Supports Older Scots/Early Scots periodization through about 1450, continuous written records from 1375-76, divergence from contemporary Middle English, and the pre-1494 name Inglis.

evidence.citation: Middle English Dictionary, entry til, quotations from '(1400) Let.Hen.IV in RS 18.1 (Vsp F.7),' p. 24.
evidence.summary: Current scholarly citation corroborating the conventional 1400 date, Rolls Series 18.1 source, Vespasian F VII siglum, and parallel Middle English lexicographic classification.

evidence.citation: British Library, 'Digitisation services - advice, scanning, printing.'
evidence.summary: Explains the paid beta order route, required shelfmark/title/folio-side/format/payment data, expert photography for rare items, inability to scan original manuscripts, possible restrictions, and permission enquiries.

evidence.citation: British Library, 'Imaging Services terms and conditions,' updated January 2026.
evidence.summary: Makes fulfillment subject to retrieval, condition, complete order data, payment, and individual agreement for difficult or large orders; places rights clearance on the user and requires written BL permission to reproduce supplied material, potentially for additional fees.

evidence.citation: UK Intellectual Property Office, 'How copyright protects your work: How long copyright lasts' and 'Copyright Notice: Duration of copyright (term).'
evidence.summary: Current official guidance for life-plus-70 literary-work terms and the 25-year term for published-edition typographical arrangements; used with the 1860 publication and Hingeston's 1910 death date, without conflating text, physical ownership, catalog prose, or images.

open_questions.question: Which side or sides of Cotton MS Vespasian F VII f. 22 carry the letter and endorsement, and do Hingeston's reported traces of a seal survive today?
next_steps: Request staff confirmation or commissioned reference photographs of f. 22 recto and verso.
Record seal condition only after current item-level inspection; do not infer it from Hingeston's 1860 report.
notes: The current record's Digitised Content field is blank and no public f. 22 image was found.

open_questions.question: Why do the CME XML and U-M text give Rymer viii. 181 where Hingeston's inspected p. 24 reads viii. 131?
next_steps: Verify the actual Foedera passage at volume VIII p. 131.
If confirmed, correct the XML in a separately scoped encoding change while preserving the current artifact's reading in lineage history.

open_questions.question: Which peerage numbering convention explains Hingeston's eleventh Earl and the current University of Edinburgh authority's tenth Earl of Dunbar and March for George Dunbar (1338-1420)?
next_steps: Consult a current specialist peerage history or national authority that explicitly explains Dunbar/March ordinal conventions.
Keep the preferred agent name ordinal-free until resolved.

open_questions.question: Why does the inherited Latin scope statement describe Cotton MS Vespasian F VII as 121 leaves while the current structured physical description reports 130 folios?
next_steps: Ask BL whether the 121-leaf statement reflects an older extent before endleaf/content-list accounting, a historical foliation, or a catalog error.
Continue to use current f. 1-130 for access and reproduction planning.

open_questions.question: What documented technical process produced the checked-in CME XML from the University of Michigan encoding?
next_steps: Seek repository history, import scripts, or maintainer confirmation before strengthening version_of to copied_from.
notes: Matching content establishes representational identity but not an exact technical copy event or direction.

open_questions.question: What item-specific reuse terms apply to the Internet Archive PDF and OCR derivatives for royalhistoricall01hing?
next_steps: Ask Internet Archive and, if necessary, PIMS-University of Toronto for an item-specific determination before redistributing the scan derivatives.
Continue to distinguish public download availability from an affirmative open license.
